original: Traditional date of Laylat al-Qadr / Night of Power when Muslims believe the Quran is first sent down from heaven to the world and revealed to the Islamic prophet Muhammad
Pope Eugene I elected to succeed Martinus I
Treaty of Verdun: Brothers Lotharius I, Louis the German, and Charles the Bald divide France
Battle of Lechfeld: Otto I, King of the Germans defeats the Hungarian, ending 50 years of Magyar invasion of Western Europe

Battle of Maldon: English, led by Earl Byrhtnoth, confront a band of inland-raiding Vikings near Maldon in Essex. The battle and the English defeat are immortalized in the well-known Old English poem "The Battle of Maldon."
Battle at Zierik Sea: Dutch and French fleet beat Flemish fleet
Second Battle of Athenry, Anglo-Norman victory over Gaelic clan alliance
Majorcan explorer Jaume Ferrer sets sail to find the legendary "river of gold" along the West African coast and disappears without a trace
Alfonso ed Espina, bishop of Osma, urges for an Inquisition in Spain
Portuguese sea captain Diego Diaz is first European to sight Madagascar
Portuguese troops occupy parts of Malacca
Battle of Saint-Mathieu (also called the Battle of Brest): an English fleet of 25 ships commanded by Sir Edward Howard and a Franco-Breton fleet of 22 ships commanded by René de Clermont
Siege of Florence ends with the city's surrender to the Spanish forces of Charles I, ending the Republic of Florence
Battle of St Quentin in Picardy: Lamoraal, Count of Egmont and Philibert, Duke of Savoy (Spanish Empire) beat the Duke of Montmorency (France)
Protestant Iconoclasm "Beeldenstorm" (Iconoclastic Fury) begins in the European Low Countries
John Mason and Ferdinando Gorges receive a land patent of the Province of Maine
Cardinal Richelieu begins the siege of La Rochelle

Battle of Scheveningen: Final naval battle of the First Anglo-Dutch War, English fleet defeats a Dutch fleet led by Maarten Tromp, who is killed by a sharpshooter in the rigging of William Penn's ship
Austria and the Ottoman Empire sign the Peace of Vásvár
Portuguese-Jewish synagogue opens in Amsterdam
Netherlands and France sign peace treaty in Nijmegen
Tewa medicine man Popé leads the Pueblo Rebellion against Spanish colonizers in the New Mexican province, killing 400 and driving out another 2,000
Earliest recorded prizefighting rules are formulated
Carlos III crowned King of Spain
Louvre Palace officially opens in Paris as the Musée Central des Arts
Area of Quito is the first to declare itself independent of Spain in Latin America, though put down by the Spanish after 24 days (now Ecuador's National Day)
Missouri is admitted as the 24th US state
Antioch, Syria, is hit by an earthquake, killing about 20,000 people
Hurricane hits Barbados, killing about 1,500 people
Mob of white people and oxen pull Black school to a swamp out of Canaan, NH
US Act of Congress passes establishment of the Smithsonian Institution, now the world's largest museum and research complex
Hurricane washes away 200-300 revelers on Last Island, Louisiana
Battle of Wilson's Creek [Battle of Oak Hills], fought near Springfield, Missouri, General Lyon is killed in a Confederate victory (US Civil War)
Battle of the Nueces River in Texas, also known as the Nueces Massacre
O. B. Brown patents a moving picture projector
First phone call between Brantford and Paris, Canada
Leo Daft opens America's first commercially operated electric streetcar in Baltimore
Excursion train crashes in Chatsworth, Illinois, killing 101 people
Dan Rylands patents the screw cap
Chinese deported from San Francisco under Exclusion Act
First Queen's Hall Promenade Concert (Wagner's "Rienzi")
Automobile Club of Great Britain is established (now called the Royal Automobile Club)
Chicago White Sox's Frank Isbell strands a record 11 teammate base runners
Famous Viking ship, the Oseberg, dating to 820 AD, is uncovered in a burial mound near Tønsberg, Norway, with two female skeletons and the bones of 15 horses
Battle of the Yellow Sea: Japanese fleet prevents Russians from breaking out of Port Arthur
Dutch newspaper "Volk" fires gay journalist Jacob de Cock

Pope Pius X condemns the 1905 French "Law on the Separation of the Churches and State," which grants the state authority to control aspects of Catholic orthodoxy and worship (Encyclical "Gravissimo Officii Munere")
Prince Scipione Borghese wins the Beijing to Paris 7,500-mile auto rally
Parliament Act reduces power of UK's House of Lords
The UK House of Commons votes on an annual salary of £400 for its members
Treaty of Bucharest ends the Second Balkan War as Bulgaria cedes territory
At Liège, German 12"/16.5" guns reach the Belgian border
German battlecruiser Goeben reaches Dardanelles; Turkey joins Germany
German troops reconquer Mulhouse in Alsace
WWI: French fall back in Alsace
Turks annex Persian city Hamadan from Russia
"Gasoline Alley" comic strip by Frank King premieres in the Chicago Tribune
Ukrainian National Army massacres 25 Jews in Podolia, Ukraine
Allies recognize Poland, Czechoslovakia, and Romania
Mamie Smith and her Jazz Hounds record “Crazy Blues” for Okeh Records, the first significant blues hit by an African American artist
The Turkish government renounces its claim to Israel and recognizes the British Mandate of Palestine
Treaty of Sèvres is signed between victorious WWI Allies and the Ottoman Empire, giving Ottoman territories to France, Greece, and Italy; replaced by Treaty of Lausanne in 1923
Hurricane strikes Borculo, Netherlands, killing four people
Italian-Spanish peace treaty is signed
Grover Alexander beats the Phillies 7-1 for his 373rd and final National League win
A 5.1 kg (11.2 pound) chondrite-type meteorite breaks into at least seven pieces and lands near the town of Archie in Cass County, Missouri
The Lego Group is founded in Billund, Denmark, by Ole Kirk Christiansen; the name is a contraction of the Danish words "leg godt" ("play well" in English)
114°F (46°C) recorded in Plain Dealing, Louisiana (state record)
120°F (49°C) recorded in Ozark, Arkansas (state record)
First time Olympic cycling road race is conducted as a mass start event with Frenchmen Robert Charpentier and Guy Lapébie taking gold and silver, and France winning the team gold medal
119°F (48°C) recorded in Pendleton, Oregon (state record)
Second Dutch De Geer government forms (first with Social Democrats)
Dutch Prince Bernhard Fund forms
200 Jews escape the Mir Ghetto in Poland
Dutch submarine attacks Hertengbeest Island, NW Bali
Battle for Guam ends with US troops recapturing Guam from the Japanese
Braves' Red Barrett throws only 58 pitches to shut out the Cincinnati Reds 2-0
Race riots in Athens, Alabama
The combined US and French offensive begins at Alencon
ABC enters network TV at 7 pm (WJZ, NY)
Allen Funt's "Candid Camera" TV debuts on ABC
US National Military Establishment renamed Department of Defense
All-time great English jockey Sir Gordon Richards retires from the saddle with a British record 4,870 winning rides
At Massena, New York, the groundbreaking ceremony for the St. Lawrence Seaway is held
Dutch Indonesian Union breaks up
23rd NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Cleveland 26, All-Stars 0 (75,000 attendees)
The US performs a nuclear test at the Nevada Test Site
Discoverer 13 launches into orbit and returns the first object from space
Nicolaas Jouwe forms PANA in New Guinea
UK applies for membership of the European Economic Community
USSR conducts a nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, USSR
WJSP TV channel 28 in Columbus, GA (PBS) begins broadcasting
USAF test pilot Joe Engle in the X-15 reaches an altitude of 51.3 miles (82.6 km) and a top speed of 3,550 mph (5,713 km/h) (Mach 5.20)
NASA launches Lunar Orbiter 1 to the Moon to photograph the lunar surface
Race riots in Miami, Chicago, and Little Rock
British Home Secretary Reginald Maudling threatens to impose direct rule on Northern Ireland if the agreed reform measures are not carried out
During the internment round-up operation in West Belfast, the Parachute Regiment kills 11 unarmed civilians in what becomes known as the Ballymurphy Massacre
Sixteen baseball researchers form the Society for American Baseball Research
A one-million-kilogram meteor grazes the atmosphere above Canada
The Great Fireball, a rare daylight meteor visible from Utah, US, to Alberta, Canada, passes 35 miles above Earth's surface
First BART train travels through Transbay Tube to Montgomery St. Station

Provisional Irish Republican Army volunteer is shot dead by the British Army as he drives in Belfast; his car goes out of control and kills three children, sparking "peace rallies" throughout the month by the 'Peace People'
About 100 white sympathizers join evicted black squatters in a protest against the demolition of shanty dwellings outside Cape Town, South Africa
Phillies and Expos play a doubleheader that ends at 3:23 am
Postal employee David Berkowitz is arrested in Yonkers, NY, accused of being the "Son of Sam" .44 caliber killer
US and Panama sign Panama Canal Zone accord
USSR performs an underground nuclear test
USSR conducts a nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya
Ecuador adopts its 17th constitution, extending citizenship and suffrage to all Ecuadorians over 18
Wings release singles "Getting Closer" and "Baby's Request"
Allen, the most powerful hurricane in the Caribbean, hits Brownsville, Texas
Coca-Cola Bottling Co. agrees to invest $34 million in Black-owned businesses
Richard Nixon Museum in San Clemente, California, closes
Famous Mary Decker-Zola Budd collision during 3000 m at the Los Angeles Olympics; Decker falls, Budd finishes 7th, and Maricica Puică of Romania wins
Ian Ferguson of New Zealand wins two canoeing gold medals in the one day, winning the K-1 500 and K-2 500 with Paul MacDonald at the Los Angeles Olympics; he wins K-4 1000 the next day
Sweden's Agneta Andersson wins the women's canoeing 500 m double at the Los Angeles Olympics with victories in the K-1 and K-2 with Anna Olsson
Uno Lindström of Sweden juggles a soccer ball 13.11 miles (21.09 km)
Marquis Theatre opens at 1535 Broadway, New York City
Flight Readiness Firing of Discovery's main engines is successful
Kevin Gross is the second pitcher in eight days to be ejected for scuffing the ball
Rodrigo Borja is installed as president of Ecuador
The UN estimates Asia's population has hit 3 billion
A's bat out of order against White Sox in the third inning
Australia is 0-301 at the end of day one of the fifth Test Cricket match at Trent Bridge
NASA's Magellan spacecraft arrives at Venus
The massacre of more than 127 Muslims in northeast Sri Lanka by paramilitaries
NFL sportscaster Paul Maguire suffers a heart attack at 53
Satellite TOPEX/Poseidon launches
Soyuz TM-15 lands
An earthquake measuring 7.0 on the Richter Scale hits the South Island of New Zealand
Last British troops leave Hong Kong; they have been there since September 1841
The Dodgers lead 2-1 in the 9th and forfeit the game to the Cardinals as fans become unruly
Dare & Go ends Cigar's record-tying victory streak at 16
New Zealand beats South Africa 29-18 in Cape Town to win the inaugural Tri Nations Rugby Series with an unbeaten record; All Blacks flyhalf Andrew Mehrtens lands 5 penalties and 2 conversions
Parlisha Williams of Louisiana is crowned Ms. Black USA Metroplex
Yankees lose, ending third-best home series victory streak at 24
Anaheim Angels' Tony Phillips is arrested for purchasing cocaine
Royal Proclamation of HRH Prince Al-Muhtadee Billah as the Crown Prince of Brunei
New Zealand beats South Africa 30-23 in Durban to win their fourth Tri Nations Rugby Series; All Blacks score four tries to two
The highest temperature ever recorded in the UK: 38.5°C (101.3°F) in Kent, the first time the UK records a temperature over 100°F
Scotland Yard disrupts a major terrorist plot to destroy aircraft traveling from the United Kingdom to the United States; all toiletries are banned from commercial aircraft
British cyclist Nicole Cooke wins the road race at the Beijing Olympics, earning Great Britain's 200th gold in the modern Olympics
Six people are killed after Paluweh volcano erupts in Indonesia
Sixteen Royal Guardsmen are killed in a bus accident in Al Hoceima, Morocco
Israeli and Palestinian officials agree to a 72-hour ceasefire proposed by Egypt
Unrest breaks out in Ferguson, Missouri, after the shooting death of African-American Michael Brown (18) by police officer Darren Wilson
110°F (43°C) in Apalachicola, Florida, breaks the state record of 109°F (42.8°C) set in 1931

South Korean shooter Jin Jong-oh recovers from 7th place during the final to set an Olympic record of 193.7 and win gold in men's 50 m pistol at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; he is the first shooter to win three consecutive Olympic gold medals in one event

Weightlifter Nijat Rahimov of Kazakhstan sets a world record with a 214 kg lift in the clean and jerk and finishes with a 379 kg total to win the gold medal in the men's 77 kg category at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics
Second Temple in Jerusalem set on fire by Roman army underTitusduring the capture of the city (approx)
ExplorerJohn Cabottells KingHenry VIIof his trip to "Asia"
EnglishQueen Elizabeth Isigns Treaty of Nonsuch: Aid for Netherlands
KingCharles IIandJohn Flamsteedlay the foundation stone of the Royal Observatory in Greenwich, London
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozartcompletes his chamber piece "Eine kleine Nachtmusik" (A Little Serenade)
Papers from the Tuileries Palace, proving theComte de Mirabeauhas secret dealings with the court, are revealed
Revolutionaries storm the Tuileries Palace in Paris, residence of the royal family

Battle of the Big Hole: US Army led by Colonel John Gibbon fights theNez Perceled byChief Josephand Looking Glass in Montana; both sides suffer heavy casualties, causing the Nez Perce to continue their long fighting retreat
NY Giant pitcherTim Keefesets a 19-game winning streak record
Rudolf Diesel's prototype internal combustion engine, a single 10-foot iron cylinder with a flywheel at its base, runs on its own power for the first time in Augsburg, Germany
German chemistFelix Hoffmannfirst synthesizes acetylsalicylic acid, which is later patented by his company Bayer under the name "aspirin"
Angelo Giuseppe Roncalli (future PopeJohn XXIII) becomes a priest
NY Highlanders pitcherJack Chesbroends string of 30 consecutive complete games in a 5-1 loss to Chicago White Sox at South Side Park, Chicago
Babe Ruthannounces this is his final season as a full-time player
Franklin D. Rooseveltand Churchill's second meeting takes place in Placentia Bay, Newfoundland
GeneralBernard Montgomeryis appointed commander of the British 8th Army in North Africa
Henry J. Kaiseropens the Kaiser Richmond Field Hospital for Kaiser Shipyards in Richmond, California, pioneering the Kaiser Permanente healthcare model
Adolf Hitlerwatches a lynching of Allied pilots
US GeneralGeorge S. Pattonslaps a battle-fatigued soldier at an evacuation hospital, calling him "cowardly," and threatens him with a pistol
Japan announces its willingness to surrender to the Allies, providedEmperor Hirohito's status remains unchanged
Ezzard CharlesTKO's Gus Lesnevich in the 8th round for the heavyweight boxing title
"Sunset Boulevard", American film noir directed byBilly Wilder, and starringWilliam HoldenandGloria Swanson, premieres at Radio City Music Hall in NYC
Los Angeles premiere ofAlfred Hitchcock's "Psycho," starringAnthony PerkinsandJanet Leigh
"Little"Stevie Wonder's single "Fingertips (Part 2)" begins a three-week run, making him the youngest singer to top the charts at 13
Don Suttonbreaks his 13-game losing streak to the Cubs with a 4-2 win
Twins'Harmon Killebrewbecomes the 10th player to amass 500 home runs and adds his 501st
Paul andLinda McCartneyand their band Wings are arrested in Gothenburg, Sweden, on drug possession charges when customs officials discover a package of hashish
Roberta Flackscores her third No. 1 single with "Feel Like Makin' Love"
David Frostpurchases exclusive rights to interview former US PresidentRichard Nixon
Elton Johnperforms the first of seven sold-out nights at Madison Square Garden, NYC, to conclude his North American tour
"Off the Wall," the fifth studio album byMichael Jackson, is released (Billboard Album of the Year 1980)
MLB Philadelphia Phillies'Pete Rose, with his 3,631st career hit, breaksStan Musial's National League hit record
The US beats Spain 96-65 to win the men's basketball gold medal at the Los Angeles Olympics; future 'dream team' membersMichael Jordan,Patrick Ewingand Chris Mullin feature
Oakland A'sDave Kingmanbecomes the 21st player to hit 400 home runs off Matt Young in Seattle
PitcherBob Forschhits a grand slam to lead the Cardinals to a 5-4 win over the Pittsburgh Pirates in St. Louis
Yankees retireBilly Martin's uniform number 1 during Billy Martin Day at Yankee Stadium
Alan Ayckbourn's play "Man of the Moment" premieres at Stephen Joseph Theatre in Scarborough
Matt Biondiswims a 100 m freestyle world record in 48.42 seconds
Columbia Records releases "River of Dreams," singer-songwriterBilly Joel's twelfth and final studio album (unless he changes his mind)
Ruth Bader Ginsburgis sworn in as a US Supreme Court Justice
Bob Dolepicks Jack Kemp as his Republican vice-presidential running mate
Atlanta Braves signGreg Madduxto a then-record 5-year, $57.5 million deal
US and UK reject a proposal by UN Secretary GeneralKofi Annanto permit the Iraqi government to use $1 billion per year to fund infrastructure improvements and to increase oil production capacity
American super-star swimmerMichael Phelpswins the 400 m individual medley at the Beijing Olympics in a world record time of 4:03.84
Google announces its restructuring as Alphabet, a holding company with Google, YouTube, Android, and Chrome as subsidiaries
Swedish warship Vasa sinks in Stockholm, killing 30
News of the United States Declaration of Independence reaches London during the American Revolutionary War

Franklin D. Rooseveltis stricken with a paralytic illness at his summer home on the Canadian island of Campobello, which is thought to be polio at the time but could possibly be Guillain-Barré syndrome
Jim Morrison's trial for "lewd and lascivious behavior" begins in Miami, Florida
Albumasar [Abu Ma‘shar al-Balkhi], Arabic astronomer, born in Balkh, Khurasan (d. 886)
John the Blind, King of Bohemia (1310-46) and Count of Luxembourg, born in Luxembourg (d. 1346)
Francesco Zabarella, Italian cardinal and jurist, born in Padua (d. 1417)
Albert II of Germany, King of Hungary, Croatia and Bohemia, born in Vienna, Duchy of Austria (d. 1439)
Jacob Sturm von Sturmeck, German statesman of the Protestant Reformation, born in Strasbourg (d. 1553)

Philipp Nicolai, German Lutheran pastor, poet, composer and hymnodist, born in Mengeringhausen, Landgraviate of Hesse, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1607)
Hieronymus Praetorius, German choral composer, born in Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1629)

Eusebio Kino, Italian Catholic missionary and explorer in Mexico and Arizona, born in Segno, Bishopric of Trent (d. 1711)

Daniel Gottlob Türk, German organist and composer, born in Claußnitz, Saxony (d. 1813)
Santiago Ferrer, Spanish composer, born in Spain (d. 1824)
Isobel Gunn, Orkney women who disguised herself as a man to work for the Hudson Bay Company (one of the first women in Western Canada), born in Tankerness, Scotland (d. 1861)[1]
August Neithardt, German military bandleader (Prussian Army, 1816-40), choirmaster (Berlin Cathedral, 1843-61), and  composer, born in Schleiz, Imperial County of Reuss, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1861)

Carl Friedrich Weitzmann, German composer, conductor, and music theorist, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1880)
Camilio Benso di Cavour, Italian politician who advocated for unification (First Prime Minister of Italy 1861), born in Turin, First French Republic (d. 1861)
Cornelis Outshoorn, Dutch architect (Amstel Hotel), born in Nieuwveen, Netherlands (d. 1875)

Jacob Edvard Gille, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1880)
John Clifford Pemberton, American lieutenant general Confederate Army charged with defending Vicksburg, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1881)
William Lowndes Yancey, American politician who defended slavery, diplomat for the Confederacy, born in Warren County, Alabama (d. 1863)
Jay Cooke, American financier, fundraiser for the Federal government during US Civil War, born in Sandusky, Ohio, (d. 1905)
Charles Keene, English artist and illustrator (worked in black and white), born in London, England (d. 1891)

Hugh Stowell Brown, Manx preacher who worked among the poor in Liverpool, born in Douglas, Isle of Man (d. 1886)
Gyula Beliczay, Hungarian pianist, composer, teacher, and engineer, born in Révkomárom, Hungary (d. 1893)

Paul Warburg, German-American investment banker who influenced and encouraged the design of the Federal Reserve, born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1932)
Laurence Binyon, British poet (For the Fallen), dramatist, and art scholar, born in Lancaster, Lancashire, England (d. 1943)
Bill Johnson, American jazz double bassist and banjo player, born in Talladega, Alabama (d. 1972) [birthdate disputed]
Tod Sloan, American thoroughbred jockey (1,000 Guineas 1899 [Sibola], Ascot Gold Cup 1900 [Merman]; created monkey crouch riding style), born in Bunker Hill, Indiana (d. 1933)

(Cecil) Armstrong Gibbs, British composer, born in Great Baddow, Essex, England (d. 1960)

Richard Reeves, American character actor (The Adventures of Superman; Date With an Angel), born in Queens, NYC, New York (d. 1967)
Romain Maes, Belgian road cyclist (Tour de France 1935), born in Zerkegem, Belgium (d. 1983)

Joseph Hardy, American character actor (Husbands; The Clock), born in Arlington, Massachusetts (d. 1990)
